AI.conclusion: Current CAC models need more semantically grounded architectures. The proposed framework provides a reliable assessment tool for future open-world text-guided CAC methods.

title: Seeing Realism from Simulation: Efficient Video Transfer for Vision-Language-Action Data Augmentation
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models typically rely on large-scale real-world videos, whereas simulated data, despite being inexpensive and highly parallelizable to collect, often suffers from a substantial visual domain gap and limited environmental diversity, resulting in weak real-world generalization. We present an efficient video augmentation framework that converts simulated VLA videos into realistic training videos while preserving task semantics and action trajectories. Our pipeline extracts structured conditions from simulation via video semantic segmentation and video captioning, rewrites captions to diversify environments, and uses a conditional video transfer model to synthesize realistic videos. To make augmentation practical at scale, we introduce a diffusion feature-reuse mechanism that reuses video tokens across adjacent timesteps to accelerate generation, and a coreset sampling strategy that identifies a compact, non-redundant subset for augmentation under limited computation. Extensive experiments on Robotwin 2.0, LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, and a real robotic platform demonstrate consistent improvements. For example, our method improves RDT-1B by 8% on Robotwin 2.0, and boosts $π_0$ by 5.1% on the more challenging LIBERO-Plus benchmark. Code is available at: https://github.com/nanfangxiansheng/Seeing-Realism-from-Simulation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a video augmentation framework that converts simulated robotic videos into realistic training videos for VLA models, addressing the domain gap between simulation and reality through semantic segmentation, caption rewriting, and conditional video generation with diffusion feature-reuse for efficiency.
AI.motivation: Simulated data is cheap and parallelizable but suffers from a visual domain gap and limited diversity, resulting in poor real-world generalization for VLA models.
AI.method: Extracts structured conditions via video semantic segmentation and captioning, rewrites captions for diversity, uses a conditional video transfer model, with a diffusion feature-reuse mechanism and coreset sampling for efficiency.
AI.result: Improves RDT-1B by 8% on Robotwin 2.0, boosts π₀ by 5.1% on LIBERO-Plus, with consistent gains across multiple benchmarks and a real robotic platform.
AI.conclusion: The proposed simulation-to-reality video augmentation framework effectively bridges the domain gap for VLA models, improving real-world generalization with practical scalability.

title: Unified Map Prior Encoder for Mapping and Planning
summary: Online mapping and end-to-end (E2E) planning in autonomous driving remain largely sensor-centric, leaving rich map priors, including HD/SD vector maps, rasterized SD maps, and satellite imagery, underused because of heterogeneity, pose drift, and inconsistent availability at test time. We present UMPE, a Unified Map Prior Encoder that can ingest any subset of four priors and fuse them with BEV features for both mapping and planning. UMPE has two branches. The vector encoder pre-aligns HD/SD polylines with a frame-wise SE(2) correction, encodes points via multi-frequency sinusoidal features, and produces polyline tokens with confidence scores. BEV queries then apply cross-attention with confidence bias, followed by normalized channel-wise gating to avoid length imbalance and softly down-weight uncertain sources. The raster encoder shares a ResNet-18 backbone conditioned by FiLM with scaling and shift at every stage, performs SE(2) micro-alignment, and injects priors through zero-initialized residual fusion, so the network starts from a do-no-harm baseline and learns to add only useful prior evidence. A vector-then-raster fusion order reflects the inductive bias of geometry first, appearance second. On nuScenes mapping, UMPE lifts MapTRv2 from 61.5 to 67.4 mAP (+5.9) and MapQR from 66.4 to 71.7 mAP (+5.3). On Argoverse2, UMPE adds +4.1 mAP over strong baselines. UMPE is compositional: when trained with all priors, it outperforms single-prior models even when only one prior is available at test time, demonstrating powerset robustness. For E2E planning with the VAD backbone on nuScenes, UMPE reduces trajectory error from 0.72 to 0.42 m L2 on average (-0.30 m) and collision rate from 0.22% to 0.12% (-0.10%), surpassing recent prior-injection methods. These results show that a unified, alignment-aware treatment of heterogeneous map priors yields better mapping and better planning.

AI.tldr: UMPE is a unified encoder that fuses four types of map priors (HD/SD vector maps, rasterized SD maps, satellite imagery) with BEV features for autonomous mapping and planning, using alignment-aware gating and fusion mechanisms. It achieves +5.9 mAP on nuScenes mapping, +4.1 mAP on Argoverse2, and reduces trajectory error by 0.30m L2 and collision rate by 0.10% on E2E planning.
AI.motivation: Existing autonomous driving mapping and planning approaches are sensor-centric and underutilize heterogeneous map priors (HD/SD vector maps, rasterized maps, satellite imagery) due to issues like pose drift, modality differences, and inconsistent availability during test time.
AI.method: UMPE has two branches: (1) a vector encoder that pre-aligns HD/SD polylines with SE(2) correction, encodes points with sinusoidal features, and uses confidence-biased cross-attention with normalized channel-wise gating; (2) a raster encoder with FiLM-conditioned ResNet-18, SE(2) micro-alignment, and zero-initialized residual fusion. Priors are fused in vector-then-raster order (geometry first, appearance second).
AI.result: On nuScenes: MapTRv2 improved from 61.5 to 67.4 mAP (+5.9), MapQR from 66.4 to 71.7 mAP (+5.3). On Argoverse2: +4.1 mAP. For E2E planning with VAD: trajectory L2 error reduced from 0.72 to 0.42m (-0.30m), collision rate from 0.22% to 0.12% (-0.10%). Demonstrates powerset robustness when only subsets of priors are available.
AI.conclusion: A unified, alignment-aware encoder that handles heterogeneous map priors compositionally can significantly improve both mapping and planning performance, outperforming prior-injection methods and showing robustness to varying prior availability at test time.

title: FoR-Net: Learning to Focus on Hard Regions for Efficient Semantic Segmentation
comment: 8 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables. Efficient semantic segmentation under resource-constrained settings. Code will be released
summary: We present FoR-Net, a lightweight architecture for semantic segmentation that focuses on identifying and enhancing hard regions. Instead of relying on heavy global modeling, FoR-Net adopts an efficient strategy that selectively emphasizes informative regions through a learned importance map and a Top-K activation mechanism. Specifically, a selector module predicts region-wise importance, enabling the model to focus on challenging areas such as thin structures and object boundaries. Multi-scale reasoning is achieved using convolutional branches with different receptive fields, allowing diverse spatial context aggregation. We evaluate FoR-Net on the Cityscapes benchmark under limited computational resources. Despite its lightweight design and standard training configuration, FoR-Net achieves competitive performance and demonstrates improved consistency in challenging regions. These results suggest that region-focused reasoning provides a simple yet effective inductive bias for efficient semantic segmentation.

AI.tldr: FoR-Net is a lightweight semantic segmentation architecture that uses a learned importance map and Top-K activation to selectively focus on hard regions like thin structures and boundaries, achieving competitive performance on Cityscapes with limited computation.
AI.motivation: To design a lightweight semantic segmentation model that efficiently focuses on challenging regions (e.g., thin structures, object boundaries) without relying on heavy global modeling.
AI.method: FoR-Net uses a selector module to predict region-wise importance, adopts a Top-K activation mechanism to emphasize informative regions, and employs multi-scale convolutional branches with different receptive fields for diverse spatial context aggregation.
AI.result: On the Cityscapes benchmark with limited computational resources, FoR-Net achieves competitive performance and demonstrates improved consistency in challenging regions.
AI.conclusion: Region-focused reasoning provides a simple yet effective inductive bias for efficient semantic segmentation, suggesting that selectively emphasizing hard regions can match the performance of heavier models.

title: TOC-SR: Task-Optimal Compact diffusion for Image Super Resolution
summary: Diffusion models have recently demonstrated strong performance for image restoration tasks, including super-resolution. However, their large model size and iterative sampling procedures make them computationally expensive for practical deployment. In this work, we present TOC-SR, a framework for building efficient one-step super-resolution models by first discovering a compact diffusion backbone. Starting from a sixteen-channel latent diffusion model, we construct parameter-efficient surrogate blocks using feature-wise generative distillation and perform architecture discovery using epsilon-constrained Bayesian Optimization to minimize model complexity while preserving generative fidelity. The resulting compact diffusion backbone achieves a 6.6x reduction in parameters and a 2.8x reduction in GMACs compared to the expanded diffusion model. We then adapt this backbone for super-resolution and distill the diffusion process into a single-step generator. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed approach enables efficient super-resolution while maintaining strong reconstruction quality.

AI.tldr: TOC-SR is a framework that creates efficient one-step super-resolution models by discovering compact diffusion backbones through generative distillation and Bayesian optimization, achieving 6.6x parameter reduction and 2.8x GMACs reduction while maintaining reconstruction quality.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models are powerful for image super-resolution but suffer from high computational costs due to large model size and iterative sampling, making them impractical for real-world deployment.
AI.method: The method involves three key steps: (1) constructing parameter-efficient surrogate blocks via feature-wise generative distillation from a 16-channel latent diffusion model, (2) performing architecture discovery using epsilon-constrained Bayesian Optimization to balance model complexity and generative fidelity, and (3) distilling the diffusion process into a single-step generator for super-resolution.
AI.result: The compact diffusion backbone achieves a 6.6x reduction in parameters and a 2.8x reduction in GMACs compared to the expanded model, while maintaining strong reconstruction quality in super-resolution tasks.
AI.conclusion: TOC-SR successfully enables efficient, one-step super-resolution by combining compact architecture discovery with diffusion process distillation, offering a practical solution for deploying diffusion-based super-resolution models.

title: Linearizing Vision Transformer with Test-Time Training
summary: While linear-complexity attention mechanisms offer a promising alternative to Softmax attention for overcoming the quadratic bottleneck, training such models from scratch remains prohibitively expensive. Inheriting weights from pretrained Transformers provides an appealing shortcut, yet the fundamental representational gap between Softmax and linear attention prevents effective weight transfer. In this work, we address this conversion challenge from two perspectives: architectural alignment and representational alignment. We identify Test-Time Training (TTT) as a linear-complexity architecture whose two-layer dynamic formulation is structurally aligned with Softmax attention, enabling direct inheritance of pretrained attention weights. To further align representational properties, including key shift-invariance and locality, we introduce key instance normalization and a lightweight locality enhancement module. We validate our approach by linearizing Stable Diffusion 3.5 and introduce SD3.5-T$^5$ (Transformer To Test Time Training). With only 1 hour of fine-tuning on 4$\times$H20 GPUs, SD3.5-T$^5$ achieves comparable text-to-image quality to the fine-tuned Softmax model, while accelerating inference by 1.32$\times$ and 1.47$\times$ at 1K and 2K resolutions.

AI.tldr: The paper converts pretrained Softmax attention Transformers (specifically Stable Diffusion 3.5) into linear-complexity TTT models via architectural and representational alignment, achieving comparable quality with 1.32-1.47x inference speedup using only 1 hour of fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Training linear-complexity attention models from scratch is prohibitively expensive, and transferring weights from pretrained Softmax Transformers fails due to the representational gap between Softmax and linear attention mechanisms.
AI.method: (1) Architectural alignment: identifies TTT as a structurally compatible linear-complexity architecture with Softmax attention due to its two-layer dynamic formulation; (2) Representational alignment: introduces key instance normalization for shift-invariance and a lightweight locality enhancement module to bridge the representational gap.
AI.result: SD3.5-T⁵ achieves comparable text-to-image quality to fine-tuned Softmax model with only 1 hour of fine-tuning on 4×H20 GPUs, while speeding up inference by 1.32× at 1K and 1.47× at 2K resolutions.
AI.conclusion: Softmax-to-linear attention conversion is feasible through careful architectural and representational alignment, enabling efficient weight inheritance without expensive training from scratch.

title: HumanSplatHMR: Closing the Loop Between Human Mesh Recovery and Gaussian Splatting Avatar
summary: Accurately recovering human pose and appearance from video is an essential component of scene reconstruction, with applications to motion capture, motion prediction, virtual reality, and digital twinning. Despite significant interest in building realistic human avatars from video, this paper demonstrates that existing methods do not accurately recover the 3D geometry of humans. ViT-based approaches are not consistently reliable and can overfit to 2D views, while NeRF- and Gaussian Splatting-based avatars treat pose and appearance separately, limiting rendering generalization to new poses. To resolve these shortcomings, this paper proposes HumanSplatHMR, a joint optimization framework that refines 3D human poses while simultaneously learning a high-fidelity avatar for novel-view and novel-pose synthesis. Our key insight is to close the loop between geometric pose estimation and differentiable rendering. Unlike prior human avatar methods that rely on accurate human pose obtained through motion capture systems or offline refinement, which are impractical in in-the-wild scenarios, our approach uses only human mesh estimates from a state-of-the-art human pose estimator to better reflect real-world conditions. Therefore, instead of using the human pose only as a deformation prior, HumanSplatHMR backpropagates photometric, segmentation, and depth losses through a differentiable renderer to the pose parameters and global position. This coupling refines the global 3D pose over time, improving accuracy and alignment while producing better renderings from novel views. Experiments show consistent improvements over pose recovery baselines that omit image-level refinement and avatar baselines that decouple pose estimation from avatar reconstruction.

AI.tldr: HumanSplatHMR jointly optimizes 3D human pose and high-fidelity avatar reconstruction from video by closing the loop between geometric pose estimation and differentiable rendering, using only mesh estimates from a pose estimator without requiring motion capture systems.
AI.motivation: Existing methods (ViT-based, NeRF/Gaussian Splatting-based) fail to accurately recover 3D human geometry — ViT overfits to 2D views, while NeRF/Gaussian approaches treat pose and appearance separately, limiting generalization to new poses.
AI.method: A joint optimization framework that backpropagates photometric, segmentation, and depth losses through a differentiable renderer to refine pose parameters and global position, using only human mesh estimates from a SOTA pose estimator (no motion capture needed).
AI.result: Consistent improvements over pose recovery baselines that omit image-level refinement and avatar baselines that decouple pose estimation from avatar reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: Closing the loop between pose estimation and differentiable rendering improves both 3D pose accuracy and novel-view/novel-pose avatar rendering quality in real-world scenarios without reliance on impractical motion capture systems.

title: Edge-Efficient Image Restoration: Transformer Distillation into State-Space Models
summary: We propose a modular framework for hybrid image restoration that integrates transformer and state-space model (SSM) blocks with a focus on improving runtime efficiency on edge hardware. While transformers provide strong global modeling through self-attention, their attention kernels incur substantial latency on mobile devices, especially for high-resolution inputs. In contrast, SSMs such as Mamba offer lineartime sequence modeling with lower runtime overhead but may underperform on fine grained restoration tasks. To balance accuracy and efficiency, we train lightweight SSM blocks as feature-distilled surrogates of transformer blocks and use them to construct hybrid U-Net-style architectures. To automatically discover effective block combinations, we introduce Efficient Network Search (ENS), a multi-objective search strategy that selects task-specific hybrid configurations from pre-aligned components. ENS optimizes restoration quality while penalizing transformer usage, serving as a lightweight proxy for latency and enabling architecture discovery without repeated hardware profiling. On a Snapdragon 8 Elite CPU, the Restormer baseline requires 10119.52 ms for inference. In contrast, ENS-discovered hybrids significantly reduce runtime: ENS-Deblurring runs in 2973 ms (3.4x faster), ENS-Deraining in 5816 ms (1.74x faster), and ENS-Denoising in 8666 ms (1.17x faster), while maintaining competitive restoration quality.

AI.tldr: A framework combining transformer and SSM blocks for efficient image restoration on edge devices, using neural architecture search (ENS) to find optimal hybrid configurations that reduce inference latency 1.17x–3.4x on Snapdragon 8 Elite while maintaining quality.
AI.motivation: Transformers are powerful for image restoration but suffer from high latency on edge hardware due to costly attention kernels. The authors aim to balance accuracy and computational efficiency by replacing transformer blocks with lightweight SSM surrogates in hybrid architectures.
AI.method: (1) Train lightweight SSM blocks (Mamba) as feature-distilled surrogates of transformer blocks. (2) Construct hybrid U-Net architectures combining SSM and transformer blocks. (3) Use Efficient Network Search (ENS), a multi-objective search that optimizes restoration quality while penalizing transformer usage as a proxy for latency, enabling architecture discovery without hardware profiling.
AI.result: On Snapdragon 8 Elite CPU: ENS-Deblurring 2973 ms (3.4x faster), ENS-Deraining 5816 ms (1.74x faster), ENS-Denoising 8666 ms (1.17x faster) vs Restormer baseline 10119.52 ms, with competitive restoration quality.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid transformer-SSM architectures discovered via multi-objective search can significantly reduce inference latency on mobile hardware while maintaining restoration performance, offering a practical solution for edge deployment.

title: IConFace: Identity-Structure Asymmetric Conditioning for Unified Reference-Aware Face Restoration
summary: Blind face restoration is highly ill-posed under severe degradation, where identity-critical details may be missing from the degraded input. Same-identity references reduce this ambiguity, but mismatched pose, expression, illumination, age, makeup, or local facial states can lead to overuse of reference appearance. We propose \textbf{IConFace}, a unified reference-aware and no-reference framework with identity--structure asymmetric conditioning. References are distilled into a norm-weighted global AdaFace identity anchor for image-only modulation, while the degraded image is reinforced as the spatial structure anchor through low-rank residuals and block-wise degraded cross-attention with two-route memory. The resulting single checkpoint exploits references when available and falls back to no-reference restoration when absent, improving identity consistency, fine-detail recovery, and degraded-only restoration quality in a unified model.

AI.tldr: IConFace is a unified framework for blind face restoration that uses identity-structure asymmetric conditioning to exploit same-identity references when available and fall back to no-reference restoration when absent, improving identity consistency and detail recovery.
AI.motivation: Blind face restoration is ill-posed under severe degradation as identity-critical details are missing. Same-identity references reduce ambiguity but can cause overuse of reference appearance due to mismatched pose, expression, illumination, etc.
AI.method: References are distilled into a norm-weighted global AdaFace identity anchor for image-only modulation, while the degraded image is reinforced as the spatial structure anchor via low-rank residuals and block-wise degraded cross-attention with two-route memory.
AI.result: A single checkpoint exploiting references when available and falling back to no-reference restoration when absent, improving identity consistency, fine-detail recovery, and degraded-only restoration quality.
AI.conclusion: The proposed asymmetric conditioning approach enables unified reference-aware and no-reference restoration, effectively leveraging references without overusing them.

title: VideoNet: A Large-Scale Dataset for Domain-Specific Action Recognition
summary: Videos are unique in their ability to capture actions which transcend multiple frames. Accordingly, for many years action recognition was the quintessential task for video understanding. Unfortunately, due to a lack of sufficiently diverse and challenging data, modern vision-language models (VLMs) are no longer evaluated on their action recognition capabilities. To revitalize action recognition in the era of VLMs, we advocate for a returned focus on domain-specific actions. To this end, we introduce VideoNet, a domain-specific action recognition benchmark covering 1,000 distinct actions from 37 domains. We begin with a multiple-choice evaluation setting, where the difference between closed and open models is stark: Gemini 3.1 Pro attains 69.9% accuracy while Qwen3-VL-8B gets a mere 45.0%. To understand why VLMs struggle on VideoNet, we relax the questions into a binary setting, where random chance is 50%. Still, Qwen achieves only 59.2% accuracy. Further relaxing the evaluation setup, we provide $k\in\{1,2,3\}$ in-context examples of the action. Some models excel in the few-shot setting, while others falter; Qwen improves $+7.0\%$, while Gemini declines $-4.8\%$. Notably, these gains fall short of the $+13.6\%$ improvement in non-expert humans when given few-shot examples. Finding that VLMs struggle to fully exploit in-context examples, we shift from test-time improvements to the training side. We collect the first large-scale training dataset for domain-specific actions, totaling nearly 500k video question-answer pairs. Fine-tuning a Molmo2-4B model on our data, we surpass all open-weight 8B models on the VideoNet benchmark.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces VideoNet, a domain-specific action recognition benchmark with 1,000 distinct actions across 37 domains, revealing that VLMs significantly underperform compared to humans, especially in few-shot settings. The authors also collect a large-scale training dataset (~500k QA pairs) and demonstrate that fine-tuning a 4B model on this data surpasses all 8B open-weight models on the benchmark.
AI.motivation: Modern VLMs lack evaluation on action recognition due to insufficient diverse data. The authors aim to revitalize action recognition by focusing on domain-specific actions, revealing gaps in VLM capabilities.
AI.method: The authors create VideoNet (1,000 actions, 37 domains), evaluate VLMs in multiple-choice and binary settings, test few-shot learning with k=1,2,3 examples, and collect ~500k video QA pairs to fine-tune a Molmo2-4B model.
AI.result: Gemini 3.1 Pro achieves 69.9% vs Qwen3-VL-8B's 45.0% in multiple-choice. In binary (50% chance baseline), Qwen gets 59.2%. Few-shot: Qwen improves +7.0%, Gemini declines -4.8%, both far below humans' +13.6%. Fine-tuned Molmo2-4B surpasses all 8B open-weight models.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specific action recognition remains a challenging gap for VLMs; training on targeted data effectively improves performance, suggesting the need for continued focus on this area in the VLM era.

title: Active Sampling for Ultra-Low-Bit-Rate Video Compression via Conditional Controlled Diffusion
summary: Diffusion models provide a powerful generative prior for perceptual reconstruction at ultra-low bitrates, but effective video compression requires controlling the generative process using highly compact conditioning signals. In this work, we present ActDiff-VC, a diffusion-based video compression framework for the ultra-low-bitrate regime. Our method partitions videos into variable-length segments, transmits keyframes only when needed, and summarizes temporal dynamics using a compact set of tracked point trajectories. Conditioned on these sparse signals, a conditional diffusion decoder synthesizes the remaining frames, enabling perceptually realistic reconstruction under severe rate constraints. To support this design, we introduce two mechanisms: content-adaptive keyframe selection and budget-aware sparse trajectory selection, which together enable compact yet effective conditioning for generative reconstruction. Experiments on the UVG and MCL-JCV benchmarks show that ActDiff-VC achieves up to 64.6\% bitrate reduction at matched NIQE, improves KID by up to 64.6\% and FID by up to 37.7\% at comparable bitrates against strong learned codecs, and delivers favorable perceptual rate--distortion trade-offs relative to learned and diffusion-based baselines in the ultra-low-bitrate regime.

AI.tldr: ActDiff-VC is a diffusion-based video compression framework for ultra-low-bitrates that partitions videos into variable-length segments, transmits keyframes selectively, and uses tracked point trajectories as conditioning signals for a diffusion decoder to reconstruct frames, achieving significant bitrate reduction and perceptual quality improvements.
AI.motivation: To enable effective video compression at ultra-low bitrates by leveraging diffusion models as generative priors, while overcoming the challenge of controlling the generative process with highly compact conditioning signals.
AI.method: 1) Partitions videos into variable-length segments; 2) Transmits keyframes only when needed; 3) Summarizes temporal dynamics using tracked point trajectories; 4) Uses content-adaptive keyframe selection and budget-aware sparse trajectory selection; 5) Conditional diffusion decoder synthesizes remaining frames.
AI.result: Up to 64.6% bitrate reduction at matched NIQE; 64.6% improvement in KID; 37.7% improvement in FID at comparable bitrates against strong learned codecs on UVG and MCL-JCV benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: ActDiff-VC delivers favorable perceptual rate-distortion trade-offs relative to learned and diffusion-based baselines in the ultra-low-bitrate regime, demonstrating the effectiveness of sparse trajectory-based conditioning for generative video reconstruction.

title: Pixel Perfect: Relational Image Quality Assessment with Spatially-Aware Distortions
summary: Traditional image quality assessment (IQA) methods rely on mean opinion scores (MOS), which are resource-intensive to collect and fail to provide interpretable, localized feedback on specific image distortions. We overcome these limitations by shifting from absolute quality prediction to a relational and directional assessment. Our approach utilizes a self-supervised synthetic distortion engine to generate training data, eliminating the need for manual annotation. A distortion prediction network is trained with an anti-symmetric objective to produce spatially-aware, disentangled maps that identify the type, intensity, and direction of distortions relative to a reference image. Subsequently, a scoring network is trained via contrastive learning on ordinally ranked image sets to predict a relational quality score. Our method provides a more granular and interpretable approach to IQA for the targeted optimization of image processing algorithms without requiring any human-labeled quality scores.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised IQA method that predicts distortion types, intensity, and direction maps plus relational quality scores without needing human-labeled MOS data.
AI.motivation: Traditional IQA relies on MOS, which is expensive to collect and lacks interpretable, localized feedback on specific distortions.
AI.method: Uses a self-supervised synthetic distortion engine for training data, a distortion prediction network with anti-symmetric objective for disentangled maps, and a scoring network via contrastive learning on ordinally ranked image sets.
AI.result: Produces spatially-aware maps identifying distortion type, intensity, and direction, plus a relational quality score.
AI.conclusion: Enables granular, interpretable IQA for targeted optimization of image processing algorithms without human-labeled quality scores.

title: Laplacian Frequency Interaction Network for Rural Thematic Road Extraction
summary: Rural thematic road network construction aims to extract topological road structures from movement trajectory images of agricultural machinery. However, this task faces challenges where downsampling methods commonly used in existing studies tend to blur the sparse high-frequency road structures, and the heavy noise from dense field operations often leads to fragmented or redundant topologies in the extracted networks. To address these challenges, we propose LFINet, a Laplacian Frequency Interaction Network. The network begins with a Laplacian Multi-scale Separator (LMS) to decouple the image into low-frequency semantic contexts and high-frequency structural details. These components are then processed by the Cross-Frequency Interaction Block (CFIB) through a dual-pathway architecture in which a High-Frequency Block (HFB) refines local structures while a Spatial Transformer (ST) captures global semantics. Subsequently, a Frequency Gated Modulation (FGM) mechanism integrates the features from pathways by leveraging semantic contexts to calibrate the structural details. Finally, a Progressive Reconstruction Decoder iteratively fuses multi-scale features to ensure topological consistency. Experiments conducted on a real-world agricultural trajectories dataset from Henan Province, China, show that LFINet establishes a new state-of-the-art. Specifically, it achieves an F1-score of 92.54% and an IoU of 86.12%, surpassing the second-ranked method by 0.64% and 1.1%, respectively. This confirms its capability to effectively construct topological road networks from noisy and sparse field data.

AI.tldr: LFINet is a Laplacian Frequency Interaction Network for rural road network construction from agricultural machinery trajectories, achieving state-of-the-art F1-score of 92.54% and IoU of 86.12% by decoupling low/high-frequency features and using cross-frequency interaction to handle noise and sparse structures.
AI.motivation: Existing downsampling methods blur sparse high-frequency road structures, and dense field operation noise causes fragmented or redundant topologies in extracted road networks from agricultural trajectory images.
AI.method: LFINet consists of: (1) Laplacian Multi-scale Separator (LMS) for decoupling low-frequency semantics and high-frequency details; (2) Cross-Frequency Interaction Block (CFIB) with dual pathways including High-Frequency Block (HFB) and Spatial Transformer (ST); (3) Frequency Gated Modulation (FGM) for integrating features; (4) Progressive Reconstruction Decoder for multi-scale feature fusion.
AI.result: LFINet achieves 92.54% F1-score and 86.12% IoU on a real-world agricultural trajectories dataset from Henan Province, China, surpassing the second-ranked method by 0.64% and 1.1% respectively.
AI.conclusion: LFINet effectively constructs topological road networks from noisy and sparse field data, establishing a new state-of-the-art for rural thematic road network construction.

title: AlbumFill: Album-Guided Reasoning and Retrieval for Personalized Image Completion
summary: Personalized image completion aims to restore occluded regions in personal photos while preserving identity and appearance. Existing methods either rely on generic inpainting models that often fail to maintain identity consistency, or assume that suitable reference images are explicitly provided. In practice, suitable references are often not explicitly provided, requiring the system to search for identity-consistent images within personal photo collections. We present AlbumFill, a training-free framework that retrieves identity-consistent references from personal albums for personalized completion. Given an occluded image and a personal album, a vision-language model infers missing semantic cues to guide composed image retrieval, and the retrieved references are used by reference-based completion models. To facilitate this task, we introduce a dataset containing 54K human-centric samples with associated album images. Experiments across multiple baselines demonstrate the difficulty of personalized completion and highlight the importance of identity-consistent reference retrieval. Project Page: https://liagm.github.io/AlbumFill/

AI.tldr: AlbumFill is a training-free framework that retrieves identity-consistent reference images from personal albums to perform personalized image completion, using a vision-language model for semantic cue inference and reference-based inpainting.
AI.motivation: Existing personalized image completion methods either fail to maintain identity consistency (generic inpainting) or assume suitable reference images are explicitly provided, which is unrealistic in practice. The authors aim to solve the problem of automatically retrieving identity-consistent references from personal photo collections.
AI.method: AlbumFill is a training-free framework that: (1) uses a vision-language model to infer missing semantic cues from the occluded image, (2) guides composed image retrieval from personal albums using those cues, and (3) feeds retrieved identity-consistent references into reference-based completion models.
AI.result: The authors introduce a 54K human-centric sample dataset with album images. Experiments across multiple baselines demonstrate the difficulty of personalized completion and highlight the importance of identity-consistent reference retrieval.
AI.conclusion: Identity-consistent reference retrieval is crucial for personalized image completion, and AlbumFill's training-free approach effectively addresses the gap where suitable reference images are not explicitly provided.
